scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Plato writes from 4th-c. Athens; his political observations are rooted in the intellectual and civic life of the Athenian poleis.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Written by Plato during his late period at the Academy (-367); the rule reflects contemporary philosophical distinctions between expertise and wealth.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The description of laws being passed by the 'people' after review by commissioners reflects the historical Athenian legislative process.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to Plato's authorship (-367); the process described is a contemporary feature of Athenian constitutional life.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The categorization of regimes based on their relationship to law was a standard exercise in 4th-century Greek political theory.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Stated by Plato at his floruit (-367); the distinction between legitimate and illegitimate rule by the rich was a live political debate.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The relationship between rhetoricians and political leaders was a central concern of Athenian civic and educational life.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to Plato's floruit (-367) as a contemporary analysis of the hierarchy of arts in his own society.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The subordination of the 'strategos' (general) to the political authorities was a fundamental tenet of Athenian democracy.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Stated by Plato in his contemporary dialogue (-367); the tension between military skill and political decision-making was evergreen in Athens.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The role of judges as servants of the law rather than creators of it reflects the legalist ideals of the 4th-century Athenian courts.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Written by Plato at his floruit (-367); the rule captures the contemporary judicial ethics expected of Athenian dikastai.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Punishments such as exile and atimia (deprivation of rights) were standard judicial instruments of the Athenian state.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to Plato's floruit (-367); the passage describes penological practices contemporary to the author's world.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The link between lack of education (or 'natural' inferiority) and slavery reflects the social and legal justifications common in 4th-c. Greece.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to Plato's floruit (-367); the text assumes the contemporary legality and social logic of the institution of slavery.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The eligibility for magistracies based on birth and education reflects the transition from aristocratic to meritocratic norms in Athens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Stated by Plato at his floruit (-367); the rule addresses the contemporary selection criteria for high state office.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The practice of forming marriage alliances for wealth and power is described as a contemporary social habit of the Athenian elite.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Recorded by Plato as an observation of his own society's marriage customs at the time of writing (-367).

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The 'texture' of the state including both slaves and freemen describes the social totality of the Athenian polis under rule of law.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Stated by Plato at his floruit (-367) as an理想ized but contemporary conception of the integrated political community.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Lucian explicitly places this dialogue in the mouth of Solon, describing the specific institutions of archaic Athens to a foreigner.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Lucian is writing retrospectively about the laws and customs established at Solon's floruit (-594).

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The official in the purple cloak overseeing athletes is part of Lucian's depiction of Solon-era Athenian state regulation.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to Solon's archonship (-594), as Lucian frames this as a historic institution described by Solon himself.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The distribution of specific prizes like the Panathenaic oil is cited by Solon as a feature of the Athenian legal and religious order.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to Solon's historical floruit (-594), representing the traditional prize system attributed to his era.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The speaker, Anacharsis, is explicitly contrasting the Athenian gymnastic violence with the laws of his own Scythian society.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to the arrival of Anacharsis in Athens (-589), when he would have been describing his native laws.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Lucian has Solon describe the procedures of the Areopagus, which was the central judicial body of early Athens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to Solon's floruit (-594); the rule represents the 'ancestral' judicial customs attributed to him.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The specific prohibition of rhetoric in the Areopagus is a famous legal tradition of the ancient Athenian murder court.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to -594, as Lucian presents this as a core feature of the Solonic legal reform.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Although spoken by Solon, the use of theatre (comedy and tragedy) for public education is a hallmark of the 5th and 4th-c. Athenian polis.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Lucian’s description of theatre as state education reflects the enduring Classical Athenian institution as understood in his own time (165).

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The license of comedians to ridicule citizens (parresia) was a specific legal and social feature of Athenian democracy.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Lucian is describing a famous historical Athenian practice that remained a topic of literary interest in the 2nd century CE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The public display of laws on 'kyrbeis' or pillars for citizens to read is a foundational act of Solon's constitutional reforms.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to the enactment of Solon's laws (-594), which first established the principle of publicly accessible written law in Athens.
